coredata.dc:title: Collision damage assessment in lithium-ion battery cells via sensor monitoring and ensemble learning
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2018 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:issueName: 6th CIRP Global Web Conference – Envisaging the future manufacturing, design, technologies and systems in innovation era (CIRPe 2018)
coredata.dc:creator: Simeone, Alessandro
Lv, Dian
Liu, Xiang Yang
Zhang, Jian
coredata.dc:description: The safety of lithium ion batteries (LIBs) is an important issue in electric vehicle industry. Collision damage characterization is an essential aspect of the overall safety assessment of electric vehicle LIBs. Although immediate consequences may not appear evident, battery cells long-term safety and performance can be seriously affected by damages resulting from collisions, leading to dangerous failures. In this paper, a framework and associated methodology for battery cells collision damage assessment is proposed. An experimental rig was designed and built for the realization of a collision tests campaign. During such tests a number of sensor signals were collected and processed to extract significant features. The collision damages were then characterized in terms of physical inspection and electrical performances. An ensemble learning based pattern recognition decision making support system was setup by inputting environmental conditions parameters and sensor signal features to assess the collision damage class and the charge/discharge ability. Classification results are discussed and hints for future developments are proposed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: lithium-ion battery
collision safety
sensors
ensemble learning

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: rawtext articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids confeditor contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight oauserlicense openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content oa subj suppl tomb vol volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast primabst ref

xocs:title-editors-group.ce:title: 6th CIRP Global Web Conference – Envisaging the future manufacturing, design, technologies and systems in innovation era (CIRPe 2018)

xocs:meta.xocs:copyright-line: © 2018 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license.

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2018 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.

xocs:doc.xocs:rawtext: ScienceDirect Available online at www.sciencedirect.com www.elsevier.com/locate/procedia Procedia CIRP 78 (2018) 273â€“278 2212-8271 Â© 2018 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V. This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/) Selection and peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of the 6th CIRP Global Web Conference â€œEnvisaging the future manufacturing, design, technologies and systems in innovation eraâ€�. 10.1016/j.procir.2018.09.073 Â© 2018 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V. This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/) Selection and peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of the 6th CIRP Global Web Conference â€œEnvisaging the future manufacturing, design, technologies and systems in innovation eraâ€�. Keywords: lithium-ion battery; collision safety; sensors; ensemble learning 1. Introduction During the last decade, the electric vehicle industry has developed rapidly. The lithium-based batteries are widely used and as a kind of good storage way in electric vehicle battery technology. The main advantages of these batteries are high energy density, light weight, fast charge, and relatively low cost [1]. Currently, research and development on lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) is focusing on improvement of specific energy and safety [2, 3]. With the increasing sales of electric vehicles, more accidents related to power batteries occurred[1, 4]. The LIB failure under external factors may cause generation of smoke, fire and even explosion [5]. Wide literature is available on battery safety, concerning thermal runaway and mechanical response, in which collision safety is an unavoidable problem [2]. Xuning Feng et al. surveyed the occurrence and possible cause of a number of electric vehicle accidents [6]. Literature review highlighted how not all the batteries are short circuited or fail immediately when a collision accident occurs. According to the damage extent in battery cells and the speed of their internal chemical reactions, the harmful effect caused by damages may not appear immediately, yielding to delayed safety hazards. This phenomenon poses some challenges when dealing with such collision vehicles issue [6]. 6th CIRP Global Web Conference â€œEnvisaging the future manufacturing, design, technologies and systems in innovation eraâ€� Collision damage assessment in lithium-ion battery cells via sensor monitoring and ensemble learning Alessandro Simeoneab, Dian Lva, XiangYang Liua, Jian Zhangab* a Intelligent Manufacturing Key Laboratory of Ministry of Education, Shantou University, Shantou, 515063,China b Shantou Institute for Light Industrial Equipment Research, Shantou University, Shantou, 515063,China * Corresponding author. Tel.: +8613502724586. E-mail address: jianzhang@stu.edu.cn Abstract The safety of lithium ion batteries (LIBs) is an important issue in electric vehicle industry. Collision damage characterization is an essential aspect of the overall safety assessment of electric vehicle LIBs. Although immediate consequences may not appear evident, battery cells long- term safety and performance can be seriously affected by damages resulting from collisions, leading to dangerous failures. In this paper, a framework and associated methodology for battery cells collision damage assessment is proposed. An experimental rig was designed and built for the realization of a collision tests campaign. During such tests a number of sensor signals were collected and processed to extract significant features. The collision damages were then characterized in terms of physical inspection and electrical performances. An ensemble learning based pattern recognition decision making support system was setup by inputting environmental conditions parameters and sensor signal features to assess the collision damage class and the charge/discharge ability. Classification results are discussed and hints for future developments are proposed. This is a resupply of March 2023 as the template used in the publication of the original article contained errors. The content of the article has remained unaffected. 274 Alessandro Simeone et al. / Procedia CIRP 78 (2018) 273â€“278 Relatively small damages, in fact, may affect the battery cell internal structure [5]. As the battery keeps working, the damage can cause phenomena such as accelerated ageing, degradation of the solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) and internal resistance increase [7, 8]. These phenomena involve many complex mechanisms, resulting in safety hazards such as battery overheat, energy loss, capacity attenuation, power reduction and thermal runaway [9â€“ 14]. As regards the battery module or pack level, non- uniformity issues can arise [15]. Consequently, minor collision-related damages which do not appear serious at the time of the event, may lead to dangerous failures with the prolonged use of the battery cell. This kind of cell can be defined as latent-danger cell. Timely detection and replacement of latent-danger cells can reduce the risk of safety-related accidents. Currently, the LIBs testing standards classify single cell test results into two categories: qualified and unqualified [16] leaving a gap for the identification of latent-danger in battery cells. To improve the safety aspect design, the following aspects need to be investigatedï¼š 1) Qualitative analysis of batteries safety related external factors. 2) Quantitative analysis of batteries safety factors 3) Design guidance for battery cells and modules based on failure mechanisms quantitative analysis. This research work aims at performing a qualitative battery cells damage assessment after collision utilizing sensor signals and ensemble learning based decision-making support system. With reference to Fig. 1, the research framework proposed in this work consists in four parts: experimental tests, data acquisition and processing, damage characterization and decision-making support system as introduced in the remainder this section and detailed in the following sections. â€¢ Experimental tests: an experimental programme of collision tests was design and performed on battery cells following a pre-treatment preparation procedure. â€¢ Data acquisition and processing: force, voltage, current signals were acquired using a number of sensing units and subsequently processed to extract significant features for further analysis. â€¢ Damage characterization: collision damages were investigated according to both physical and electrical perspectives. â€¢ Decision-making support system: signal features and damage labelled data were inputted into an ensemble learning pattern classifier for battery cells damage assessment. 2. Experimental procedure An experimental campaign of collision tests was carried out on brand new LiCoO2-18650 battery cells in discharge state, whose properties are reported in Table 1. The new batteries need a pre-treatment to form a stable SEI layer. The formation of SEI will affect the performance of the battery [18]. In this respect, six standard charge-discharge cycles procedure were applied to the batteries with 0.5 C- charge and 1C-discharge (charging in 1075mA and discharging in 2150 mA). 2.1. Experimental setup An experimental rig was designed and realized to allow for the cylinder-shaped stainless-steel mass to fall onto the battery cell placed in the fixture. A 3D model of the experimental rig is shown in Fig. 2. When the baffle (Fig. 2b) in the holder is removed, the mass drops and collides with the battery cell. The sensing units utilized in such experimental tests include an impact force sensor (DYLF-102 DAYSENSOR) and a voltage and current sensor (BTS 5V6A-08) for signal acquisition and processing. The force signal instances were logged by the single chip microcomputer (SCM) and sampled at 538.4 Hz. The voltage and current signals were recorded at 1 Hz. 2.2. Collision tests Table 1. 18650 LIB properties. Terms Specification Typical capacity 2150 mAh (0.2C discharge) Minimum Capacity 2050 mAh (0.2C discharge) Charging Current Standard charge: 1075mA (0.5C) Rapid charge: 2150mA (1C) Nominal voltage 3.62 V Charge voltage 4.2 V Â± 0.05 V Cut-off voltage 2.75 V Maximum charge current 2150 mA Maximum discharge current 10A (Continuous) Operating temperature Charge : 0 to 45 â„ƒ Discharge: -20 to 60 Fig. 1 Framework of Lithium-ion battery damage assessment Author name / Procedia CIRP 00 (2018) 000â€“000 3 The collision tests were carried out by dropping down a metal cylinder on the battery cell sample. In this respect, four different mass values were used, namely 2 kg, 4 kg, 6 kg and 10 kg. The drop height was set to 1 m for all the tests. Following severe collisions, the battery pack case may be subject to serious geometrical deformations, which can modify the collision interface between the case and the battery cell. In order to investigate these different conditions, two collision interfaces were utilized in this experimental campaign (see Fig. 2c), namely interface Type 1 and Type 2. These profiles are characterized by two different equations, i.e. ð�‘¦ð�‘¦ = 0.03ð�‘¥ð�‘¥2 and ð�‘¦ð�‘¦ = ð�‘¥ð�‘¥2, respectively, as shown in Fig. 3. The experimental programme, obtained by varying interface types and mass values, is reported in Table 2. In order to improve the statistical reliability of the experimental results, 6 repetitions were carried out for each collision test. Since the LIBs mechanical properties are affected by the state of charge (SOC) [19], in order to reduce the problem dimensionality, and therefore the experimental programme, the collision tests were carried out exactly after 60 seconds of discharge. 3. Signal processing and features extraction 3.1. Signal processing Collision force signals were subject to a processing procedure, aimed at extracting significant features for signal characterization [20]. The signal processing procedure is described as follows and an instance of raw signal along with its related processed signal are shown in Fig. 4: Table 2. Experimental programme 1. Performing a signal segmentation procedure to remove signal head and tail which do not contain useful information [20]. Segmented signal is shown in Fig. 4a. 2. The segmented signal is unbiased to minimize the sensor drift phenomena 3. A signal fitting procedure is then applied to the unbiased signal by using cubic spline interpolation [21] adopting 20000 query points (Fig. 4b). 3.2. Features extraction The environmental condition features including collision interface type and theoretical initial collision energy. â€¢ Collision interface type ð�‘‡ð�‘‡ indicated with 0 and 1 for type 1 and type 2 respectively. â€¢ Theoretical initial collision energy E defined in Eq. 1: ð��¸ð��¸ = ð�‘šð�‘šð�‘šð�‘šâ„Ž (1) Where m is the mass of dropping cylinder and h is the falling height and g is the acceleration of gravity. Statistical features were extracted from the collision force signals: peak value (ð��¹ð��¹ð�‘šð�‘šð�‘šð�‘šð�‘šð�‘š ) mean value (ð��¹ð��¹ð�‘šð�‘šð�‘šð�‘šð�‘šð�‘šð�‘šð�‘š )ï¼Œ variance (ð��¹ð��¹ð�‘£ð�‘£ð�‘šð�‘šð�‘£ð�‘£)ï¼Œ skewness (ð��¹ð��¹ð�‘ ð�‘ ð�‘ ð�‘ ð�‘šð�‘šð�‘ ð�‘ ) and kurtosis (ð��¹ð��¹ð�‘ ð�‘ ð�‘˜ð�‘˜ð�‘£ð�‘£ð�‘˜ð�‘˜). 4. Battery cell damage characterization The battery cells were divided into 3 classes according to damage types and entity after the collision, namely class A, B and C. [22, 23] as explained below: â€¢ Class A - safe cells. After the collision test, cells are defined safe whether they show enough maximum power and can discharge efficiently to keep device working according to specifications. (a) (b) (c) Fig. 2 Collision tests experimental rig 3D-model (a); cylinder setting (b); battery positioning (c) ID Interface type Mass (kg) Repetitions 1 Type 1 4 6 2 Type 1 6 6 3 Type 1 10 6 4 Type 2 2 6 5 Type 2 4 6 6 Type 2 6 6 (a) Interface type 1 (b) Interface type 2 Fig. 3. Interface profiles: ð�‘¦ð�‘¦ = 0.03ð�‘¥ð�‘¥2 (a) ð�‘¦ð�‘¦ = ð�‘¥ð�‘¥2(b) This is a resupply of March 2023 as the template used in the publication of the original article contained errors. The content of the article has remained unaffected. Alessandro Simeone et al. / Procedia CIRP 78 (2018) 273â€“278 275 2 Author name / Procedia CIRP 00 (2018) 000â€“000 Relatively small damages, in fact, may affect the battery cell internal structure [5]. As the battery keeps working, the damage can cause phenomena such as accelerated ageing, degradation of the solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) and internal resistance increase [7, 8]. These phenomena involve many complex mechanisms, resulting in safety hazards such as battery overheat, energy loss, capacity attenuation, power reduction and thermal runaway [9â€“ 14]. As regards the battery module or pack level, non- uniformity issues can arise [15]. Consequently, minor collision-related damages which do not appear serious at the time of the event, may lead to dangerous failures with the prolonged use of the battery cell. This kind of cell can be defined as latent-danger cell. Timely detection and replacement of latent-danger cells can reduce the risk of safety-related accidents. Currently, the LIBs testing standards classify single cell test results into two categories: qualified and unqualified [16] leaving a gap for the identification of latent-danger in battery cells. To improve the safety aspect design, the following aspects need to be investigatedï¼š 1) Qualitative analysis of batteries safety related external factors. 2) Quantitative analysis of batteries safety factors 3) Design guidance for battery cells and modules based on failure mechanisms quantitative analysis. This research work aims at performing a qualitative battery cells damage assessment after collision utilizing sensor signals and ensemble learning based decision-making support system. With reference to Fig. 1, the research framework proposed in this work consists in four parts: experimental tests, data acquisition and processing, damage characterization and decision-making support system as introduced in the remainder this section and detailed in the following sections. â€¢ Experimental tests: an experimental programme of collision tests was design and performed on battery cells following a pre-treatment preparation procedure. â€¢ Data acquisition and processing: force, voltage, current signals were acquired using a number of sensing units and subsequently processed to extract significant features for further analysis. â€¢ Damage characterization: collision damages were investigated according to both physical and electrical perspectives. â€¢ Decision-making support system: signal features and damage labelled data were inputted into an ensemble learning pattern classifier for battery cells damage assessment. 2. Experimental procedure An experimental campaign of collision tests was carried out on brand new LiCoO2-18650 battery cells in discharge state, whose properties are reported in Table 1. The new batteries need a pre-treatment to form a stable SEI layer. The formation of SEI will affect the performance of the battery [18]. In this respect, six standard charge-discharge cycles procedure were applied to the batteries with 0.5 C- charge and 1C-discharge (charging in 1075mA and discharging in 2150 mA). 2.1. Experimental setup An experimental rig was designed and realized to allow for the cylinder-shaped stainless-steel mass to fall onto the battery cell placed in the fixture. A 3D model of the experimental rig is shown in Fig. 2. When the baffle (Fig. 2b) in the holder is removed, the mass drops and collides with the battery cell. The sensing units utilized in such experimental tests include an impact force sensor (DYLF-102 DAYSENSOR) and a voltage and current sensor (BTS 5V6A-08) for signal acquisition and processing. The force signal instances were logged by the single chip microcomputer (SCM) and sampled at 538.4 Hz. The voltage and current signals were recorded at 1 Hz. 2.2. Collision tests Table 1. 18650 LIB properties. Terms Specification Typical capacity 2150 mAh (0.2C discharge) Minimum Capacity 2050 mAh (0.2C discharge) Charging Current Standard charge: 1075mA (0.5C) Rapid charge: 2150mA (1C) Nominal voltage 3.62 V Charge voltage 4.2 V Â± 0.05 V Cut-off voltage 2.75 V Maximum charge current 2150 mA Maximum discharge current 10A (Continuous) Operating temperature Charge : 0 to 45 â„ƒ Discharge: -20 to 60 Fig. 1 Framework of Lithium-ion battery damage assessment The collision tests were carried out by dropping down a metal cylinder on the battery cell sample. In this respect, four different mass values were used, namely 2 kg, 4 kg, 6 kg and 10 kg. The drop height was set to 1 m for all the tests. Following severe collisions, the battery pack case may be subject to serious geometrical deformations, which can modify the collision interface between the case and the battery cell. In order to investigate these different conditions, two collision interfaces were utilized in this experimental campaign (see Fig. 2c), namely interface Type 1 and Type 2. These profiles are characterized by two different equations, i.e. ð�‘¦ð�‘¦ = 0.03ð�‘¥ð�‘¥2 and ð�‘¦ð�‘¦ = ð�‘¥ð�‘¥2, respectively, as shown in Fig. 3. The experimental programme, obtained by varying interface types and mass values, is reported in Table 2. In order to improve the statistical reliability of the experimental results, 6 repetitions were carried out for each collision test. Since the LIBs mechanical properties are affected by the state of charge (SOC) [19], in order to reduce the problem dimensionality, and therefore the experimental programme, the collision tests were carried out exactly after 60 seconds of discharge. 3. Signal processing and features extraction 3.1. Signal processing Collision force signals were subject to a processing procedure, aimed at extracting significant features for signal characterization [20]. The signal processing procedure is described as follows and an instance of raw signal along with its related processed signal are shown in Fig. 4: Table 2. Experimental programme 1. Performing a signal segmentation procedure to remove signal head and tail which do not contain useful information [20]. Segmented signal is shown in Fig. 4a. 2. The segmented signal is unbiased to minimize the sensor drift phenomena 3. A signal fitting procedure is then applied to the unbiased signal by using cubic spline interpolation [21] adopting 20000 query points (Fig. 4b). 3.2. Features extraction The environmental condition features including collision interface type and theoretical initial collision energy. â€¢ Collision interface type ð�‘‡ð�‘‡ indicated with 0 and 1 for type 1 and type 2 respectively. â€¢ Theoretical initial collision energy E defined in Eq. 1: ð��¸ð��¸ = ð�‘šð�‘šð�‘šð�‘šâ„Ž (1) Where m is the mass of dropping cylinder and h is the falling height and g is the acceleration of gravity. Statistical features were extracted from the collision force signals: peak value (ð��¹ð��¹ð�‘šð�‘šð�‘šð�‘šð�‘šð�‘š ) mean value (ð��¹ð��¹ð�‘šð�‘šð�‘šð�‘šð�‘šð�‘šð�‘šð�‘š )ï¼Œ variance (ð��¹ð��¹ð�‘£ð�‘£ð�‘šð�‘šð�‘£ð�‘£)ï¼Œ skewness (ð��¹ð��¹ð�‘ ð�‘ ð�‘ ð�‘ ð�‘šð�‘šð�‘ ð�‘ ) and kurtosis (ð��¹ð��¹ð�‘ ð�‘ ð�‘˜ð�‘˜ð�‘£ð�‘£ð�‘˜ð�‘˜). 4. Battery cell damage characterization The battery cells were divided into 3 classes according to damage types and entity after the collision, namely class A, B and C. [22, 23] as explained below: â€¢ Class A - safe cells. After the collision test, cells are defined safe whether they show enough maximum power and can discharge efficiently to keep device working according to specifications. (a) (b) (c) Fig. 2 Collision tests experimental rig 3D-model (a); cylinder setting (b); battery positioning (c) ID Interface type Mass (kg) Repetitions 1 Type 1 4 6 2 Type 1 6 6 3 Type 1 10 6 4 Type 2 2 6 5 Type 2 4 6 6 Type 2 6 6 (a) Interface type 1 (b) Interface type 2 Fig. 3. Interface profiles: ð�‘¦ð�‘¦ = 0.03ð�‘¥ð�‘¥2 (a) ð�‘¦ð�‘¦ = ð�‘¥ð�‘¥2(b) This is a resupply of March 2023 as the template used in the publication of the original article contained errors. The content of the article has remained unaffected. 276 Alessandro Simeone et al. / Procedia CIRP 78 (2018) 273â€“278 (a) (b) Fig. 4. Raw signal (a) and processed signal (b) â€¢ Class B - latent danger cells. Such cells are characterized by a reduced performance, in terms of battery capacity, power and useful life. Such batteries are more prone to failure than normal ones, causing safety problems. â€¢ Class C - unsafe cells. Cells are defined unsafe if they are not able to charge and discharge anymore, including those cells having presenting electrolyte leakage, burned or exploded under collision tests. The Three-class battery assessment method consists in 6 battery state evaluation characterizations and related class. The battery state evaluation characterizations include electrolyte leakage, breakage, external deformation, discharge continuity, 3C-rated capacity and internal resistance level. Electrolyte leakage, breakage, external deformation are 3 different types of physical damage form, of which instance were reported in Fig. 5. Discharge continuity indicates the ability of a battery cell to discharge continuously. Such property was observed by letting the battery fully discharge (1C rate) after collision. Battery cells able to discharge until reaching the cut-off voltage were considered to have passed the test. Two examples of voltage and current discharge signal instances are shown in Fig. 6. Battery cells which passed the discharge continuity test are then subject to a 3C-rated cycles test, with the aim of measuring the 3C-rated discharge capacity. The 3C-rated cycles consist in charging at 1C (2150 mA) and discharging at 3C (6450 mA) for 8 times. The capacity of those batteries that failed the discharge continuity test was recorded as 0. The internal resistance of sample battery cells was measured in full discharge state using a battery internal resistance meter (YR1030). The following variables are considered to formulate the specific battery cells damage class evaluation rules: â€¢ Electrolyte leakage levels: 0 for no liquid electrolyte leakage and 1 for liquid electrolyte leakage existence; â€¢ Breakage levels: 0 for no external breakage and 1 for external breakage existence; â€¢ Deformation levels: 0 for no deformations, 1 for deformations; â€¢ Discharge continuity levels: 0 or 1 respectively for passing or failing the discharge continuity test; â€¢ Internal resistance levels: 0 for internal resistance less than 35mÎ© and 1 for internal resistance more than 35mÎ©; â€¢ 3C-rated discharge capacity levels:0 for 3C-rated discharge capacity mean value more than 1860 mAh (>86.51% of Typical Capacity) and 1 for 3C-rated discharge capacity mean value less than 1860 mAh (<86.51% of Typical Capacity). A second classification problem is investigated in this paper according to whether the battery cells can effectively complete charge-discharge cycles after the collision. In this respect, Class A and class B are merged into a new class â€œA+Bâ€�. In this work, battery cells belonging to class A+B were able to charge- discharge for at least 8 cycles, while class C battery cells were not. This classification reflects the ability of the battery to discharge effectively for a period of time after the collision. The assessment methods are summarized in Table 3 where â€œ*â€� indicates that the related values do not affect the class results. 5. Intelligent decision-making support system Environmental condition and signal features were grouped in 7-element feature vectors [24] and inputted to an Ensemble Learning [25] decision making support system for damage classification purpose. Two ensemble methods were adopted in this work: RUSBoost and GentleBoost, respectively. RUSBoost [26] is an algorithm to handle class imbalance problem in data with discrete class labels. It uses a combination of random under-sampling (RUS) and AdaBoost [27] to better model the minority class by removing majority class samples. (a) (b) (c) Fig. 5. Battery cells physical damages: electrolyte lekage (a), breakage (b) and external deformation (c) Author name / Procedia CIRP 00 (2018) 000â€“000 5 GentleBoost is an improved AdaBoost algorithm, which uses real valued features and it is faster than the convergence of AdaBoost [28]. As a learner type, the decision tree technique was utilized. A decision tree can efficiently infer a tree-like classifier model from a labeled dataset, whose paths from root to leaf represent classification rules [29]. The input feature vectors for classification problems include environmental conditions parameters and force signal features: â€¢ Environmental conditions parameters:ð�‘‡ð�‘‡, ð��¸ð��¸. â€¢ Force signal features: ð��¹ð��¹ð�‘šð�‘šð�‘šð�‘šð�‘šð�‘š, ð��¹ð��¹ð�‘šð�‘šð�‘šð�‘šð�‘šð�‘šð�‘šð�‘š, ð��¹ð��¹ð�‘£ð�‘£ð�‘šð�‘šð�‘£ð�‘£, ð��¹ð��¹ð�‘ ð�‘ ð�‘ ð�‘ ð�‘šð�‘šð�‘ ð�‘ , ð��¹ð��¹ð�‘ ð�‘ ð�‘˜ð�‘˜ð�‘£ð�‘£ð�‘˜ð�‘˜. The target vectors consist in a coded value associated to the battery damage class according to the classification purpose as follows: â€¢ Three-class classification: A, B, C; â€¢ Two-class classification, A+B, C. 6. Results and discussion Fig. 7 shows the ensemble learning success rate (SR), i.e. correct classifications rate, for battery cells collision damage classification (Class A, B, C). The algorithm was configured utilizing the following parameters: â€¢ Ensemble learning method: RUSBoost â€¢ Maximum number of splits:10 â€¢ Number of learners:10 â€¢ Learning rate :0.3 â€¢ Subspace dimension: 4 Table 3.Battery assessment method The SR for this classification problem resulted equal to 86.1%, showing a very good classification performance for class A and C. (87% and 93% respectively). The SR for class B resulted sensibly lower (71%), mainly due to the small number of class B samples resulted from the experiments. Fig. 8 reports the ensemble learning success rate (SR) for charge/discharge ability classification (Class A+B, C). The algorithm was configured utilizing the following parameters: â€¢ Ensemble learning method: GentleBoost â€¢ Maximum number of splits:35 â€¢ Number of learners:30 â€¢ Learning rate :0.1 â€¢ Subspace dimension: 1 The overall SR resulted equal to 94.4% demonstrating a great suitability of sensor signal features and ensemble learning paradigm to perform pattern recognition tasks on battery cells charge/discharge ability. Clearly, the two-class classification problem is much simpler than the three-class one, for which further investigation is needed, along with a wider experimental dataset. 7. Conclusions and future work An experimental campaign of collision tests was carried out on LiCoO2 -18650 battery cells with the aim of collision damage assessment. Collision force signals were acquired, and a signal processing procedure was applied to extract significant features. Battery cells physical damages were verified and cells performance was tested in order to verify the battery discharge Fig. 6. Two voltage and current signal instances in discharge continuity tests. Instance 1 failed the test while instance 2 passed. Fig. 7 Three-class classification confusion matrix Fig. 8 Two-class classification confusion matrix Case State levels Class Leakage Breakage Deformation Continuity Capacity internal resistance 1 1 * * * * * C 2 * 1 * * * * C 3 0 0 * 1 * * C 4 0 0 * 0 1 1 C 5 0 0 1 0 0 1 B 6 0 0 1 0 1 0 B 7 0 0 1 0 0 0 A 8 0 0 0 0 0 1 B 9 0 0 0 0 1 0 B This is a resupply of March 2023 as the template used in the publication of the original article contained errors. The content of the article has remained unaffected. Alessandro Simeone et al. / Procedia CIRP 78 (2018) 273â€“278 277 4 Author name / Procedia CIRP 00 (2018) 000â€“000 (a) (b) Fig. 4. Raw signal (a) and processed signal (b) â€¢ Class B - latent danger cells. Such cells are characterized by a reduced performance, in terms of battery capacity, power and useful life. Such batteries are more prone to failure than normal ones, causing safety problems. â€¢ Class C - unsafe cells. Cells are defined unsafe if they are not able to charge and discharge anymore, including those cells having presenting electrolyte leakage, burned or exploded under collision tests. The Three-class battery assessment method consists in 6 battery state evaluation characterizations and related class. The battery state evaluation characterizations include electrolyte leakage, breakage, external deformation, discharge continuity, 3C-rated capacity and internal resistance level. Electrolyte leakage, breakage, external deformation are 3 different types of physical damage form, of which instance were reported in Fig. 5. Discharge continuity indicates the ability of a battery cell to discharge continuously. Such property was observed by letting the battery fully discharge (1C rate) after collision. Battery cells able to discharge until reaching the cut-off voltage were considered to have passed the test. Two examples of voltage and current discharge signal instances are shown in Fig. 6. Battery cells which passed the discharge continuity test are then subject to a 3C-rated cycles test, with the aim of measuring the 3C-rated discharge capacity. The 3C-rated cycles consist in charging at 1C (2150 mA) and discharging at 3C (6450 mA) for 8 times. The capacity of those batteries that failed the discharge continuity test was recorded as 0. The internal resistance of sample battery cells was measured in full discharge state using a battery internal resistance meter (YR1030). The following variables are considered to formulate the specific battery cells damage class evaluation rules: â€¢ Electrolyte leakage levels: 0 for no liquid electrolyte leakage and 1 for liquid electrolyte leakage existence; â€¢ Breakage levels: 0 for no external breakage and 1 for external breakage existence; â€¢ Deformation levels: 0 for no deformations, 1 for deformations; â€¢ Discharge continuity levels: 0 or 1 respectively for passing or failing the discharge continuity test; â€¢ Internal resistance levels: 0 for internal resistance less than 35mÎ© and 1 for internal resistance more than 35mÎ©; â€¢ 3C-rated discharge capacity levels:0 for 3C-rated discharge capacity mean value more than 1860 mAh (>86.51% of Typical Capacity) and 1 for 3C-rated discharge capacity mean value less than 1860 mAh (<86.51% of Typical Capacity). A second classification problem is investigated in this paper according to whether the battery cells can effectively complete charge-discharge cycles after the collision. In this respect, Class A and class B are merged into a new class â€œA+Bâ€�. In this work, battery cells belonging to class A+B were able to charge- discharge for at least 8 cycles, while class C battery cells were not. This classification reflects the ability of the battery to discharge effectively for a period of time after the collision. The assessment methods are summarized in Table 3 where â€œ*â€� indicates that the related values do not affect the class results. 5. Intelligent decision-making support system Environmental condition and signal features were grouped in 7-element feature vectors [24] and inputted to an Ensemble Learning [25] decision making support system for damage classification purpose. Two ensemble methods were adopted in this work: RUSBoost and GentleBoost, respectively. RUSBoost [26] is an algorithm to handle class imbalance problem in data with discrete class labels. It uses a combination of random under-sampling (RUS) and AdaBoost [27] to better model the minority class by removing majority class samples. (a) (b) (c) Fig. 5. Battery cells physical damages: electrolyte lekage (a), breakage (b) and external deformation (c) GentleBoost is an improved AdaBoost algorithm, which uses real valued features and it is faster than the convergence of AdaBoost [28]. As a learner type, the decision tree technique was utilized. A decision tree can efficiently infer a tree-like classifier model from a labeled dataset, whose paths from root to leaf represent classification rules [29]. The input feature vectors for classification problems include environmental conditions parameters and force signal features: â€¢ Environmental conditions parameters:ð�‘‡ð�‘‡, ð��¸ð��¸. â€¢ Force signal features: ð��¹ð��¹ð�‘šð�‘šð�‘šð�‘šð�‘šð�‘š, ð��¹ð��¹ð�‘šð�‘šð�‘šð�‘šð�‘šð�‘šð�‘šð�‘š, ð��¹ð��¹ð�‘£ð�‘£ð�‘šð�‘šð�‘£ð�‘£, ð��¹ð��¹ð�‘ ð�‘ ð�‘ ð�‘ ð�‘šð�‘šð�‘ ð�‘ , ð��¹ð��¹ð�‘ ð�‘ ð�‘˜ð�‘˜ð�‘£ð�‘£ð�‘˜ð�‘˜. The target vectors consist in a coded value associated to the battery damage class according to the classification purpose as follows: â€¢ Three-class classification: A, B, C; â€¢ Two-class classification, A+B, C. 6. Results and discussion Fig. 7 shows the ensemble learning success rate (SR), i.e. correct classifications rate, for battery cells collision damage classification (Class A, B, C). The algorithm was configured utilizing the following parameters: â€¢ Ensemble learning method: RUSBoost â€¢ Maximum number of splits:10 â€¢ Number of learners:10 â€¢ Learning rate :0.3 â€¢ Subspace dimension: 4 Table 3.Battery assessment method The SR for this classification problem resulted equal to 86.1%, showing a very good classification performance for class A and C. (87% and 93% respectively). The SR for class B resulted sensibly lower (71%), mainly due to the small number of class B samples resulted from the experiments. Fig. 8 reports the ensemble learning success rate (SR) for charge/discharge ability classification (Class A+B, C). The algorithm was configured utilizing the following parameters: â€¢ Ensemble learning method: GentleBoost â€¢ Maximum number of splits:35 â€¢ Number of learners:30 â€¢ Learning rate :0.1 â€¢ Subspace dimension: 1 The overall SR resulted equal to 94.4% demonstrating a great suitability of sensor signal features and ensemble learning paradigm to perform pattern recognition tasks on battery cells charge/discharge ability. Clearly, the two-class classification problem is much simpler than the three-class one, for which further investigation is needed, along with a wider experimental dataset. 7. Conclusions and future work An experimental campaign of collision tests was carried out on LiCoO2 -18650 battery cells with the aim of collision damage assessment. Collision force signals were acquired, and a signal processing procedure was applied to extract significant features. Battery cells physical damages were verified and cells performance was tested in order to verify the battery discharge Fig. 6. Two voltage and current signal instances in discharge continuity tests. Instance 1 failed the test while instance 2 passed. Fig. 7 Three-class classification confusion matrix Fig. 8 Two-class classification confusion matrix Case State levels Class Leakage Breakage Deformation Continuity Capacity internal resistance 1 1 * * * * * C 2 * 1 * * * * C 3 0 0 * 1 * * C 4 0 0 * 0 1 1 C 5 0 0 1 0 0 1 B 6 0 0 1 0 1 0 B 7 0 0 1 0 0 0 A 8 0 0 0 0 0 1 B 9 0 0 0 0 1 0 B This is a resupply of March 2023 as the template used in the publication of the original article contained errors. The content of the article has remained unaffected. 278 Alessandro Simeone et al. / Procedia CIRP 78 (2018) 273â€“278 continuity and to measure the battery capacity and internal resistance after the collision. Signal features were grouped to build input feature vectors. The feature vectors included experimental condition features (collision interface type, theoretical initial collision energy) and signal features (maximum value, mean, variance, skewness, kurtosis). Two different classification problems were set up, consisting in a three-class (collision damage classification) and a two-class (charge/discharge ability classification). These battery class assessment methods were proposed by evaluation of physical damage and electrical performance. An ensemble learning paradigm was used for pattern recognition on battery damage assessment via the environmental conditions and collision signals. Future work will involve, battery deformation and internal failure mechanisms. More experimental variables will be considered to characterize the battery damage, such as collision velocity, temperature and humidity. Acknowledgements The authors wish to acknowledge that this research has been supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51505269), the Sailing Talent Project and Pearl River Scholar Project of Guangdong Province, China, and the Science and Technologies Program (No: 2017B090922008) of Guangdong Province, China for providing financial supports to this research. The state of understanding of

head.ce:title: Collision damage assessment in lithium-ion battery cells via sensor monitoring and ensemble learning

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Intelligent Manufacturing Key Laboratory of Ministry of Education, Shantou University, Shantou, 515063, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Intelligent Manufacturing Key Laboratory of Ministry of Education

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shantou Institute for Light Industrial Equipment Research, Shantou University, Shantou, 515063, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shantou Institute for Light Industrial Equipment Research

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The safety of lithium ion batteries (LIBs) is an important issue in electric vehicle industry. Collision damage characterization is an essential aspect of the overall safety assessment of electric vehicle LIBs. Although immediate consequences may not appear evident, battery cells long-term safety and performance can be seriously affected by damages resulting from collisions, leading to dangerous failures. In this paper, a framework and associated methodology for battery cells collision damage assessment is proposed. An experimental rig was designed and built for the realization of a collision tests campaign. During such tests a number of sensor signals were collected and processed to extract significant features. The collision damages were then characterized in terms of physical inspection and electrical performances. An ensemble learning based pattern recognition decision making support system was setup by inputting environmental conditions parameters and sensor signal features to assess the collision damage class and the charge/discharge ability. Classification results are discussed and hints for future developments are proposed.
